summary: Gruber criticizes iOS 9's new default behavior of showing lowercase letters on keyboard keys when not shifted, a change that matches Android and Windows Phone. He finds the case-shifting visually distracting and cheap-looking, and explains how to disable it via an oddly-placed Accessibility setting. He argues that the real problem was iOS 7's ambiguous Shift key, not the lack of case-shifting, and notes that Apple did improve the Shift key design in iOS 9 independently. He also briefly discusses the new option to disable character preview popups on iPhone.
key_points: iOS 9 defaults to showing lowercase key caps when typing lowercase, matching Android and Windows Phone, but this can be disabled in Accessibility settings
The case-shifting keyboard is not the proper fix for iOS 7's ambiguous Shift key — an unambiguous Shift key indicator is the real solution, and these are separate issues
Apple did independently improve the Shift key in iOS 9 with clearer visual states: hollow arrow for off, solid black arrow on white for shifted, underscored arrow for Caps Lock
The setting to disable case-shifting is inexplicably located in Accessibility rather than Keyboard settings
iOS 9 also adds the option to disable character preview popups on iPhone, though Gruber found he was too habituated to them after eight years
money_quotes: I dislike it because having the keys change automatically while I'm looking at the keyboard makes it look like it's vibrating or flickering.
To me it's distracting and looks a little cheap, and if it were up to me, it wouldn't be the default.
The proper solution to an ambiguous Shift key is to replace it with an unambiguous Shift key.
The lack of case shifting on the keyboard was not a problem on iOS 1-6 because the Shift key on the old keyboard was unambiguous.
Whether you prefer a case-shifting keyboard or not, the Shift key should be unambiguous. These are two different things.
With previews off, it feels to me like the keyboard isn't working.
key_insight: Case-shifting keyboard keys are a band-aid for a different problem — the real fix for an ambiguous Shift key is a better Shift key, not changing every letter on the keyboard.
